Title: A balance between glycitein and glyceollins governed by isoflavone 6-hydroxylase confers soybean resistance to Phytophthora sojae
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: antioxidant; soybean defense against Phytophthora sojae; potential human nutraceutical benefit
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: mature roots; hypocotyls of germinating seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: daidzein 6-hydroxylation branch leading to glycitein-type isoflavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes 6-hydroxylation of daidzein to form 6-hydroxydaidzein, which is subsequently 6-O-methylated to glycitein; also hydroxylates formononetin and biochanin A in yeast assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for glycitein-type isoflavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Predominantly governs glycitein production; loss nearly abolishes glycitein-type isoflavonoids, while overexpression increases 6-hydroxydaidzein derivatives and disturbs glycitein/glyceollin balance, increasing susceptibility to P. sojae
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mGWAS; soybean hairy-root overexpression; yeast substrate-feeding assay; microsomal enzyme assay and kinetics; subcellular localization; CRISPR-Cas9 knockout; stable-isotope tracing with daidzein-d6; metabolite profiling; pathogen infection assay; chemical complementation with glycitein
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Preferentially expressed in mature roots and hypocotyls of soybean seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Preferentially expressed in mature roots and hypocotyls of 2-d-old germinating seedlings; not induced or repressed by Phytophthora sojae infection
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phytophthora sojae infection; daidzein-d6 feeding; metal ions in vitro (Mn2+ and Ca2+ enhance activity; Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ inhibit); optimal pH 7.0 and 28°C
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: IFS; 2HID; possible metabolon involvement with GmI2'H (proposed, not directly validated)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Both loss-of-function and overexpression increase susceptibility to P. sojae; overexpression diverts flux away from glyceollin I biosynthesis; glycitein-deficient material increases daidzein-type isoflavonoids ~1.5-fold in germinating seeds
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-qToF-MS metabolomics; LC-QQQ-MS/MS isotope-labeling metabolite profiling; mGWAS using soybean resequencing SNP data from previous study
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Tune rather than simply maximize GmIF6H1 expression to balance glycitein-type isoflavonoids and glyceollins for disease resistance; haplotype selection/editing at residue 248 may modulate glycitein content; glycitein-deficient lines may also be useful for tailoring human-health isoflavone profiles
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Originally named GmCYP76F17 and redesigned as GmIF6H1. A domestication-associated T248A substitution differentiates Hap2 (Thr248, higher activity) from Hap1 (Ala248, lower activity). In planta evidence supports daidzein rather than liquiritigenin as the bona fide substrate. Trace glycitein in infected mutants suggests possible minor alternative routes under specific conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. mGWAS identified GmIF6H1/Glyma.11g108300 as the strongest locus associated with glycitein-type isoflavonoids. 2. Biochemical assays showed GmIF6H1 is an ER-localized CYP76F17 isoflavone 6-hydroxylase that converts daidzein to 6-hydroxydaidzein. 3. CRISPR mutants nearly lost glycitein-type isoflavonoids, while isotope tracing confirmed daidzein is the direct in planta substrate for glycitein biosynthesis. 4. Hap2 carrying Thr248 has markedly higher catalytic efficiency than Hap1 carrying Ala248 and explains domestication-associated glycitein reduction. 5. Proper GmIF6H1 dosage is essential for defense because both deficiency and overexpression increase P. sojae susceptibility by unbalancing glycitein and glyceollin pathways.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: inducible phytoalexin for defense against Phytophthora sojae
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes daidzein 2'-hydroxylation in the glyceollin biosynthetic route
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts in the competing daidzein-centered branch that produces glyceollins; reduced glyceollin I in GmIF6H1 overexpression lines implies flux competition with GmIF6H1 during pathogen defense
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolite profiling during P. sojae infection; pathway model/inference from this study
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced upon P. sojae infection as part of glyceollin synthesis genes (described collectively)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced during pathogen infection (collective pathway-gene statement)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Possible functional perturbation by excess GmIF6H1 protein individually or within a protein complex (hypothesized)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competition with GmIF6H1 for daidzein allocation may influence balance between phytoanticipin and phytoalexin defenses
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS metabolite profiling; transcriptome profiling of pathway genes during infection
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Should be considered jointly with GmIF6H1 when engineering disease resistance to maintain balanced flux between glycitein and glyceollins
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This gene is included as a core pathway comparator in the Results/Discussion defense model, but it was not directly cloned or functionally manipulated in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The study defines GmI2'H as the enzyme directing daidzein into glyceollin biosynthesis. 2. Glyceollins function as inducible phytoalexins complementary to glycitein-type phytoanticipins. 3. GmIF6H1 overexpression lowers glyceollin I accumulation during P. sojae infection, implying competitive flux partitioning between GmIF6H1 and GmI2'H.
